title: Lesson 425
transcript: Let's spend a few minutes here talking about different backup storage strategies. Backup storage strategies is really about where organizations determine where the recovery data is going to be stored, how many copies they're going to keep, so that if something bad does happen, they're able to actually recover their data. A good example of this that a lot of organizations will use, which you don't need to memorize this, but this is sort of a rule that a lot of organizations will use. It's called the 3-2-1 rule, right? Three copies of critical files, two backups on different media. So different storage media in case there's obviously an issue with your tape drive system or something. And one backup stored offsite. This is not a universal rule. This is going to vary for organization, how critical their data is. And it's also going to depend on the data. You're not going to treat all data the same across the organization. This is where your data classification comes in, determining how valuable the data is, which is then going to drive what your backup and storage strategies are for how many copies you need to keep of critical data on how many different types of media and how many different geographically remote locations. Another aspect of data storage strategy locations is the actual location where you're storing the data. So let's go through a couple of options here. So onsite, on premise, this is where the organization is storing the backup data locally. And this has the advantage of being able to quickly recover the data onto, say, the primary system. But it has the major downside with on-site storage is that if your primary data center burns to the ground, your primary system burns to the ground, and your backups are only on-site backups, you're going to lose everything. So you typically do not want to have only on-site backups. You might use on-site backups if you need to recover some data, like if you've got a database and you need to recover it quickly to a secondary database that's on-site, you might keep an on-site copy for sort of quick recovery of data. But you don't typically want to just keep your storage copy on-site. You typically want to copy also of the backup data offsite. And there's a term that I'll introduce here and I'll talk about more later as well, and that's geographically remote. So when we're talking about offsite backups, what you typically want is you want your offsite backup to be geographically remote. And as I'll talk about more later, geographically remote is not any set distance. It's just far enough away that whatever disaster has befallen your primary site or system, it doesn't take out your offsite backup. So that's a really good idea with offsite backups, have them geographically remote at somewhere far enough away that if your primary site is taken out, it's not going to take out your backups. And what a lot of organizations are moving towards nowadays is using the cloud as their backup, because this can be a very cost-effective solution rather than having to have like a separate physical location with tape libraries and people there, you know, switching out tapes, you know, at night to be able to handle the different backups, et cetera, or have really expensive automated tape libraries. you could just back up your data in the cloud. And with the major cloud providers providing all sorts of different availability zones around the planet, you can store your data at geographically remote locations around the world and even use different major cloud service providers in case one service provider even goes down entirely. There's a bunch of advantages of cloud backups. It's highly scalable, so you can store a little bit or a ton of data very easily, can provide very high availability, and it's pay as you go. So if you, for some reason, don't need to be backing up some data for a while, you're not going to be paying anything. If you do need to store a bunch of backups for a while, you're only going to pay for what you use. It's one of the reasons a lot of organizations are moving towards using the cloud as their backup storage location.
